name: Breakneck Ridge
walkNote: Platform drops you directly at the base of the trail
seasonNote: Breakneck Ridge stop runs weekends/holidays only, April–November
desc: One of the most popular hikes in the Northeast — a steep, exposed rock scramble with panoramic Hudson River views from the summit.
tips: Trains fill up fast on nice weekends. Bring gloves for the scramble. Return from Cold Spring station (~2 mi south via trail or road).

name: Harriman — Pine Meadow Lake Loop
walkNote: Flat walk through Sloatsburg village to park entrance on Seven Lakes Dr
desc: A classic Harriman loop through mixed hardwood forest to a remote backcountry lake. Multiple connector trails let you shorten or extend the route.
tips: Short Line buses depart Port Authority Bus Terminal hourly on weekends. Buy tickets in the terminal or online.

name: Hook Mountain — Long Path Ridge
walkNote: Pleasant flat walk through Nyack village to Rockland Lake State Park trailhead
desc: Rocky Hudson palisade ridgeline with sweeping views north and south. The Long Path hugs the escarpment with several exposed overlooks.
tips: Buses to Nyack run ~45 min from Port Authority. Stop for food in Nyack on the way back — great restaurant scene.

name: Delaware Water Gap — Dunnfield Creek
walkNote: Short walk through town to the trailhead parking area at I-80 exit
desc: Lush creek-side trail through old-growth hemlock forest leading to alpine meadows and the Appalachian Trail ridge.
tips: Transbridge Lines from Port Authority, ~90 min. Confirm the last return bus time before you go — evening service is limited.

name: Sterling Forest — Appalachian Trail
walkNote: Short walk from Tuxedo station platform to AT trailhead crossing
desc: Miles of Appalachian Trail through Sterling Forest — quiet lakes, forested ridges, and frequent wildlife sightings. Far less crowded than Harriman.
tips: NJ Transit Port Jervis Line to Tuxedo runs from Penn Station. Check weekend schedule — service is less frequent than weekdays.

name: Ramapo Valley Reservation
walkNote: Flat walk or rideshare from Mahwah station along RT-202 to reservation entrance
desc: Forested hills with a scenic lake loop, rocky ridgelines, and remnants of old iron mine operations. Excellent spring birding.
tips: One of the fastest train rides — Mahwah is only 50 min from Penn. Good option for a post-work evening hike in summer.

name: South Beacon Mountain
walkNote: Walk from Beacon station along Fishkill Creek trail to Mt. Beacon trailhead
desc: A demanding loop combining the Beacon-Fishkill trail system with South Beacon summit views. Less trafficked than Breakneck with equally dramatic Hudson panoramas.
tips: Regular Hudson Line service to Beacon year-round — no seasonal limitations. Beacon has excellent post-hike food and the Dia:Beacon museum nearby.

name: Fire Island — Sunken Forest
walkNote: Ferry docks at Sailors Haven — boardwalk trail starts steps from the dock
seasonNote: Fire Island Ferry runs May–October only
desc: A magical holly forest growing behind Atlantic barrier dunes, accessible only by ferry. Flat boardwalk trail through impossibly dense canopy. Perfect for families.
tips: LIRR to Bay Shore, then Fire Island Ferries to Sailors Haven. Combine with an afternoon on the ocean beach. Ferry adds ~$15 round trip.

name: Caumsett State Park
walkNote: No transit to the park gate — 3.5 mi road walk, bike, or rideshare from Cold Spring Harbor
desc: A peaceful preserve on a Long Island Sound peninsula with coastal bluffs, salt marshes, meadows, and almost no elevation gain.
tips: A folding bike or Uber from Cold Spring Harbor makes the access much easier. The park itself is flat and uncrowded.

name: Fahnestock — Hubbard-Perkins Loop
walkNote: Rideshare strongly recommended — 2 mi from Towners station to park trailhead
desc: Remote backcountry loop through hardwood forest, past glacial ponds and rocky ridges. Exceptional solitude — far fewer crowds than Harriman or Breakneck.
tips: Worth calling a rideshare from Towners to the trailhead. Bring a paper map — cell service is unreliable inside the park.

name: Harriman — Pine Meadow / Kakiat Loop
walkNote: Walk from Suffern station north on NY-59 to Kakiat County Park trailhead
desc: Access southern Harriman via Suffern with the Kakiat Trail, connecting to the Pine Meadow Lake area. A quieter entry point than Sloatsburg.
tips: Good NJ Transit frequency to Suffern. The Kakiat trailhead is a straightforward walk from the station.

name: Black Rock Forest — Stillman Trail
walkNote: Rideshare recommended from station to Black Rock Forest trailhead (3 mi)
desc: A research forest consortium open to the public, with rocky summits, open ledges, and superb Hudson Valley views. Lightly visited.
tips: Port Jervis Line to Salisbury Mills–Cornwall. Rideshare to trailhead is easiest. The forest has excellent trail maps on their website.

name: Catskills — Kaaterskill Falls
walkNote: Short walk from bus stop to Kaaterskill Falls trailhead on Route 23A
desc: New York's tallest waterfall (260 ft) in a dramatic gorge. The escarpment trail above offers sweeping Catskill views beloved by Hudson River School painters.
tips: Trailways NY from Port Authority, ~2.5 hrs. Limited service — check schedules carefully and confirm the return bus. Worth combining with an overnight in the Catskills.

name: Harriman — Seven Hills Trail
walkNote: Walk from Tuxedo station west on Eagle Valley Rd to Harriman trail entrance
desc: A demanding traverse of Harriman's western ridges, crossing seven distinct summits with open views throughout. A true long-day adventure from the city.
tips: Plan the shuttle carefully — you can exit at multiple points. Tuxedo station on both ends makes a satisfying loop possible.

name: Watchung Reservation
walkNote: Walk or rideshare from Westfield station north to reservation trailhead
desc: A wooded trap rock ridge with a loop trail through quiet beech and oak forest. A genuine escape that's hard to believe is 45 minutes from Penn Station.
tips: Raritan Valley Line trains are reliable and frequent on weekends. Very dog-friendly park.

name: High Point State Park
walkNote: Walk from park entrance to monument trailhead along park road
desc: New Jersey's highest point at 1,803 ft, topped by a 220-ft stone monument. The AT runs through the park with sweeping tri-state views.
tips: Requires NJ Transit train + bus connection. Best done as a long day or overnight. Confirm bus connections at Hamburg.

name: Pelham Bay Park — Hunter Island
line: New Haven Line (or Subway 6 to Pelham Bay Park)
walkNote: Walk or take bus Bx29 from Pelham Bay Park subway to Hunter Island trailhead
desc: A rocky, forested peninsula in NYC's largest park — feels nothing like the city. Lagoon views, salt marshes, and an old-growth coastal forest.
tips: Closest transit hike on this list — only 35 min from midtown. Great for a quick weekday evening hike in summer.

name: Mianus River Gorge
walkNote: Rideshare recommended from Bedford Hills station to preserve entrance
desc: A Nature Conservancy preserve protecting one of the Northeast's oldest old-growth forest remnants. Quiet gorge trail with ancient hemlocks and a clear river.
tips: One of the most serene hikes near NYC. Rideshare from Bedford Hills is easiest. The preserve has a small parking area but no facilities.

name: Catskills — Giant Ledge & Panther Mountain
walkNote: Rideshare or taxi from Phoenicia to Woodland Valley trailhead (~$15)
desc: Giant Ledge offers one of the most dramatic open-ledge views in the Catskills. Continue to Panther summit for a full day adventure.
tips: Trailways from Port Authority to Phoenicia, ~2 hrs. Local taxi to Woodland Valley trailhead. Worth an overnight in Phoenicia — great basecamp town.

name: Cold Spring — Bull Hill (Mt. Taurus)
walkNote: Short walk from Cold Spring station through town to Hudson Highlands trailhead
desc: A rugged climb to Bull Hill summit with arguably the finest Hudson River views in the Highlands. The descent through Cornish Estate ruins adds a haunting historical layer.
tips: Cold Spring station has year-round regular service. The village has great cafes and a riverside park — perfect for pre or post-hike.
